speaker: interviewee
text: The other thing I learned is that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right there and then, you sit down, you don't get up anymore. Perhaps you get up this day, or the next, but altogether, it means you're giving up. You're giving in, you're giving up. Now, there's no guarantee that if you don't get up, I mean, if you stand, that you're going to make it. But these are signs, right in the beginning, where you make a conscious or subconscious decision to fight at all. It doesn't matter what happens. So then you do the best you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these-- I heard later on, these Greeks were treated awful, worse than anybody. They and the gypsies. Why? I can't tell you. Everybody said they were stealing, which they were, but then everybody was. And they were dirty and full of lice. Everybody was. 30,000 had come from [INAUDIBLE]. Can you imagine? There were not more than a few hundred in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when I saw also-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while all this was going on, and the SS guards were throwing those little bowls after them, I saw one young Greek woman, naked, sitting against one of those barracks, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] giggling to herself, and looking around sheepishly, drinking her own milk. A young mother. She had killed her baby herself, her newborn baby, before they could kill it. And now, she had, of course, gone mad, like most people were in there, including the guards. And now she drank her own milk, and looked around like this, giggling very happily, thinking that nobody should steal that milk from her. And that's when you know you're not-- you are in something which can never be described, because the worst insanity asylum is sane compared to that. Guards were drunk all the time. Guards couldn't count, which means you had to stand for hours, and hours, and hours, because they were too stupid to count. They played Russian roulette there. Not with a gun, it would've been too simple. They lined you up for the famous appell, to be counted. And then one of the guards, who had perhaps bad night, or no night at all, would say, every second, every fourth, every five, out, into the gas chambers. Which means you never knew where to stand. Or the whole row, or only the first row, or only the third. You played musical chairs all the time. But these were the things in between which happened. I was in a block. There was the-- 30,000 from the famous, famous-- I mean, I did know about it before. Bensberg, a ghetto. There were only a few hundred in there. The rest went up in flames. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What went on in the camp, in the barracks itself-- you hid under thin blankets full of lice. You fought for blankets full of lice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was never any light. It was always dark, because there were practically no light and no windows. There were many, many, many, much too many people. What you got was stupid coffee, which wasn't coffee, and bread which the blockova or the blockalteste stole anyway from you, and exchanged against ration in the other camp, in the nicer camp, where the girls were, the political were, or the non-Jews were. We had a blockova, Ilse. She was a lesbian from Berlin, 22-year-old, a real butch, who stole our ration, and then exchanged it in the other camp to buy herself some girlfriends. And then she had an assistant, which was 26-year-old Katja from Slovakia. A former professor in university, spoke five languages, was totally mad. Ilsa never hit, she just didn't want to stay in this camp. She just spent most of her time in the other camp. She let Katja do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Katja was worse than any SS guard, because she was angry, she was mad. No, she was not angry, she was mad. She had to watch her parents, her husband, and her three-year-old son be pushed in the ovens. So wouldn't you go mad? I remember I asked her once, I said, why are you doing this? We are like you. We're only prisoners. She said, you think I give a damn? My husband, my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any jobs there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the beginning, wonderful. One of those intelligent jobs, you know. Push, push cart to one end of this particular camp, full of squares of so-called grass. It wasn't real grass. And then you emptied out, and then you marched back. And then you get another load, and then you march, and then you're back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's the orchestra, and they're singing nice songs, always five in a row in front of you. Every morning, every night. They're there to lift the morale. The questions is, whose morale? So you march, just to push cart. Very hard. 12, 14 hours a day. Whoever drops, you leave them there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went all the way, pretty close to the electrical fence. They didn't like this very much. But you know where I was, there was no grass ever. No grass grown. I didn't see children play, or, or see children, for that matter. I did this for several weeks. And then one day, Ilse, the blockova-- I don't even know how or why-- asked me whether I could sing. I'd been asked whether I could sing wherever I was. I don't know why me, because people couldn't know that I sing. And I said yes. And she had outside the blockova, a little room-- oh, I don't know, perhaps like this. You know, enough for a small bed, and a little night table, and a chair. And there, she would invite me in, and I would sing for hours for her. Whatever I could think of. It was very strange. This tough little butch cried. And I even got her to stop stealing from us. She stole then from somebody else, from another block.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told her, you want me to sing? First of all, no more push cart for me. And second, you've got to stop stealing from these people. I mean, they are like you. They also want to survive. At least, some of them wanted to survive. And she did. She stole somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I don't know how many people notice about Auschwitz, that talk about warehouses full of goods, and things like this-- do you know that Auschwitz was the richest place on Earth? In the other camp-- I call it few gold pieces further-- after the working colonies were guarded, there was the business center. There was a market. And there, they sold diamonds for, for carrots, and, and 50 English pounds for, for a piece of bread. Everybody stole. I mean, in other words, you stole your own jewels, and sold them for the price of another day. And if you lived, you stole and sold again. How did you have jewels?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. They stole it. They took it from us. It wasn't a warehouse. Somebody stole it, brought it to the market. Not I. The one in the other camp. The one with the nice clean aprons. They knew-- they were in charge of their effects, as they say. Well, the effects were-- it was all brought in. It was all supposed to go to Berlin. But, of course, it didn't, right? There were fortunes there. Now, my coat, my rich, rich coat, went all the way to Auschwitz. And there, I lost it. So my jewels were in there too. So I also stole my own jewels and sold them for the price of another day. Whoever lived, stole and sold again. Now, there was one-- even the commander, the commander, he stole too. The only child I saw in my camp-- there were other too, but I've never seen a child there-- was a beautiful five-year-old gypsy boy whom the kommandant had taken from a group of gypsies. With him, he had the best toys, the best food. He drove around on his motorbike, Mr. Kommandant, in a white, little fur coat in summer, in August, all through the camp, having a ball. He had everything a little child can have, except his parents. So you see, the kommandant stole too. He stole the little gypsy boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, absolutely not. I have no idea. You had very little time to find out what happened to somebody else, really. You know, it was a moment to moment thing. They had a show there too, by the way. I didn't participate. They had-- the Polish, most of the Polish, put on a show in that little round thing where they had the coffee, what they called coffee, thing. And there was one night when they had-- they sang folk songs from their shtetl, things like this. There was a mother and twins. She was in the middle. And they sang-- I don't know whether you know it, it used to be a very famous song-- 'Yiddishe Momme,' Sophie Tucker. And even so, I used to have it in my repertoire, long before. I've never heard it, not even from Sophie Tucker, singed that way. Never. Than these two children, about 14, 15-year-old twins, sang the 'Yiddishe Momme.' And just before the end, an SS guard walked in, and took out the mother. The next day, the bookkeeper in the block, who had to cross off the names every day of those who didn't live anymore, told me that an order had come that everyone over 40 had to go into the gas chambers. It was the mother's 40th birthday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, she knew, and the children. They kept-- she walked out beautiful. No tears, very, very straight. And the children finished the song. The same night, I looked out this little cubby hole. At night, it was very, very eerie. It was very quiet, usually. The street was very empty. And there, I heard-- it must have been past midnight-- trucks. And when you hear nothing, the sound of moving trucks in the stillness is very dominating. And it was the children from the Warsaw ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was their last journey. They were all ended up in the gas chambers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From then on, I never looked out again. Never again. My friend Reina, of course, got typhus and died there. Through Ilse, I was able to get to see her in the revier, in the hospital, at all. I remember I brought her soup that didn't stink, but she was no longer-- she was in a sheet with somebody who was already dead. It was so strange, like, like this dead girl, whoever she was, was waiting for her. They were in the same sheet. And the other strange thing is that you really don't mourn anyone who dies in a camp. Not if the pain is too much, you know. [CRYING] I think you just wonder where God is in all of this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] especially for someone like Reina who did believe in him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You wonder, and you go home. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The other thing you have to watch there is you get these sores, which is, as I learned later, lack of vitamins, lack of food. It's very dangerous. It starts with-- you know, normally, you get a little scratch and it heals. But that doesn't heal. It becomes bigger, and bigger, and it becomes a big hole. And that means, already, this is the-- well, the next step is you can't go to revier. You don't know whether you'll come out again, or minus two legs, or minus whatever. So you don't go near. You can't get sick there. You don't go near there, anywhere. No matter what, you have to hide them, because you don't want anybody to see them. And I was very, very glad that everybody had them. I figured, oh gosh, because this spread like, like wildfire. It's, it's really-- they spread. Suddenly, I saw them above my-- you know, right here. So I hid with this terrible woolen dress. And then there was another one, and I hid that. But then I got one on my foot. And that is very worrisome, because you know, once that starts, it-- there's nothing you can do except good food, except good vitamins. And I didn't tell anyone about it. This is the one thing you just can't afford. You just cover up as, as, as best as you can. A matter of fact, I had one where you can now, after 50 years, still see the hole in here. It was almost gangrene, it was almost-- I would have -- if I would-- if the war wouldn't have been over when it was over, I would have been minus one leg, definitely. No doubt about it. I was that short of losing it. Because I never went to a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, what else can I tell you about Auschwitz? Nobody is normal there. Did you know that a lot of the guards were sent there as a punishment, if they had done something else in another camp, which was not according to whatever the rules were? The name Auschwitz-- I found this out much, much later-- was as dreaded by the guards than it was for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You said Auschwitz. First of all, there was silence. You know, you come from Auschwitz-- silence. Among the guards, among the other prisoners in other camps. And if you said somebody was sent to Auschwitz because whatever he did-- absolute terror. The climate was impossible, the conditions were impossible, so therefore, you could not stay there at any length and remain completely sane.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there long, and I couldn't have lasted very long. I was there short of two months. And believe me, I would not have, because eventually my sores would have been too, too many not to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? How did you get out of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day out of the blue, totally out of the blue, an SS guard came over to the block I was in, and told Betty, the other from Reina, and me to follow her. And we followed her to the other camp, where the, the better class prisoners were. The ones with the clean aprons, and with the-- so on. We were put in a block, no idea what or why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was really terrified, because they told me I could take a shower. Well, I didn't want to take a shower at that time. But it was a shower alone. I got soap, I got a washrag, I got a-- and the bunks were, were with sheets, and with pillowcases. I mean, it was not the French Riviera, but it was compared to where I had come from. And there were not Jewish people in this block. Not that I asked, are you Jewish? But, I mean, I could tell. They all had jobs in the offices, and things like this. I don't know. And I was there for one day and one night. And on the second day, I was called with Betty and 78 other women. I have no idea from which block they came, which camp they were in. I mean, Auschwitz, but Birkenau or what? I don't know. And we were put on an open truck, and were told we are going-- we are being transferred to another camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the truck was open. And, I mean, you know, if somebody tells you you're going to get out of Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's the best thing anybody can tell you. There is nothing, nothing, nothing better than that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know we drove along the camp street. And sudden-- no, the truck was not open. I'm sorry. There was one of those canvas roofs. And suddenly, the truck stopped. And someone in front-- sitting in the back, but in front-- said, gas chambers. They're taking us to the gas chambers. And it had stopped right in front of one of the ovens. And I know that I died then. I literally died. My heart stopped, and there is no light at the end of the tunnel. There's nothing but pitch darkness, and total, total horror. I cannot tell you how long it lasted, but I was sure this was it. And I was just falling down into a black hole. And then the trucks moved again. And I have no idea why they stopped there, what the reason was, whether this was a joke of the driver, or whether he just-- I don't know. And then we drove out of the camp, which I never saw exit, to a track of railroad things, something similar to where we had arrived. It was early in the morning, and there were a few very, very tired soldiers standing, yawning, and they let us out. And we waited for a few hours. And then a train came. And we were told to go on the train, but we couldn't sit together. Then they had guards with-- what for, I don't know. We already thought we were going with a shaven head, and there's a number on our arm, and these ridiculous clothes, without any permits to traveling, or food things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the train was pretty crowded with civilians. And they looked ghastly, and I was absolutely delighted. First of all, they looked at us-- do they have to be in here? You know, I mean, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: The train drove for a long time, through all the bombed out cities. This is the first time that I saw, thank God, somebody is bombing these creeps. Bombed out cities. The train stopped. Detour after detour after detour. The Germans fled out of the train, back in the train, out of the train. The train stopped all night long. It never occurred to me that they could bomb the train too, where I was sitting. I was so delighted to see nothing but rubble.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? About what time is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. The end of the summer of '43. September. Something like that. And then we drove, and drove, and drove. And then we were stopped somewhere, and marched somewhere through peaceful-looking forests. Again, always five in a row. And then we ended for another gate, another camp. And the gate opened, and we were marched through, and lined up in front of what turned out to be the sauna later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a group of many, many, many prisoners standing in prison dresses, looking at us like we were fallen from, I don't know, hell, or heaven, or something like that. And I remember when we were standing there, one girl, in a prison dress, dark hair, dark eyes, stood there-- and, you know, fashion model, foot out, really fashion model-- and said to me, do you play bridge? And I said, another madhouse. They put me into another madhouse. Turned out I got to know her very well. She was Czechoslovakian.

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp was Ravensbruck. The largest women concentration camp in Germany. It was supposed to be 25,000 capacity to-- what ended was 100,000. Eighty kilometers from Berlin. Eventually liberated, eventually liberated by the Russians. Ravensbruck was not a Jewish camp. It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you name it, it was their camp. They had-- I was quarantined-- we were quarantined again for 30 days. And the one who slept next to me, scout's honor, was there to be rehabilitated. She was not Jewish, but she had killed three husbands. I swear. Axed them to death. Now, the moral code at that time in Germany was no whores, no pimps, no lesbian, no homosexual. No, no, no, no, no. No thieves, no murderers. They were sent, as crazy as it may sound, after they had served some prison time, to Ravensbruck for rehabilitation. In this camp were murderers, thieves, of course, a lot of prostitutes, a lot of lesbians. Unfortunately, also, the Jehovah's Witnesses. Oh my God, this is the worst bunch of all. They ride around us, preaching, morning, noon, and night, the end of the world. Never of Hitler, only of the world. From morning till night. Sing song, sing song. Now these had the best jobs, where the effects were, where the clothes were. They could have been free if they would have signed a paper that they wouldn't preach against Hitler. They refused to do this. Now, they also didn't stand appell, you know, like we had to stand to be counted. They sat in the Jehovah position. Where this-- in front of the blocks, in the Jehovah position, and sang. The guards had to pick them up like this and carry them through to wherever the appell was being held. Didn't matter. Nobody could do anything with them. They stuck together like glue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had the murderers and the prostitutes. We had the Jehovah's Witnesses. We had a lot of high ranking political prisoners. I found out later that the gold clique, niece, sister, the gold was there too. A lot of high ranking political prisoners. And we had-- they didn't mix with us-- the women Russian army, the parachutes. Because there were no DP camps for women. So they were there, and they were fantastic. They didn't mix with us. They didn't talk to us. I'm sure they didn't even know where they were. They had special guards for them. They were at the end of the camp, very separated. But they took it out on them. They marched them, they marched them, they marched them, they marched them. They gave them the hardest jobs. They never-- they never, never, never-- I mean, they sang their Russian war songs, or whatever it is. Very, very brave, very, very proud of it. Absolutely fabulous. Do you know why you were sent there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I have a hunch. After it was all over, I found out about a few days before I got out of the camp-- I never knew then. I didn't know for almost two years why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you sent there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the papers from my Hungarian-bought Aryan father, who followed me from, from Amsterdam to Belgium, from Belgium to Auschwitz, were sent to Ravensbruck. And toward the end of the war, after two years there, in bunker, an underground bunker, and all of the rest, I was called to what they called the political office. And a German officer there, smiling, whispered in my ear, your father finally came through to you, didn't-- through for you, didn't he? Hand me the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you have to realize, for two years, I had no idea what the name of this man was. If somebody would have asked me, so what is your father's name, this non-Jewish father? Not a clue. The first time I saw these papers, which I have today-- original, in Hungarian, in German, with all the stamps of the Nazis on there. That's when I saw them. Hagazy, Hagazy. Good thing I-- [LAUGHS] I didn't know his name. I think this was the reason. It's the only thing I can think of. How long were you in Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost two years. From September, from the first or something, September '43, all through '44, till the end of April '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had many jobs there. My first job was very good. Digging potatoes. I'm not good at that at all. You had to march out with a big shovel. I couldn't hold that thing. It always kept falling back, hit the other one on the head, the one behind me. And then digging in potato fields. And a prisoner, also a lesbian, who was a [INAUDIBLE] [? arbiter, ?] or what you say, you know, in charge of the column, watched me and said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What did you do on the outside? Like this is the only job in the world, digging potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after three weeks, she demoted me, and I went back into the camp. What did I do? As little as possible, I know. I, I I just know I started singing again. Not for the Nazis anymore, but for the prisoners. And had a very good friend there, made a very good friend, who was also one of the 80 who was with me, shipped from Auschwitz to Ravensbruck. Eva. She collected the money. You know, a bit of toilet paper, a bit of soap. And what happened to your, your-- the typhus? How did you recover?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have typhus. I never had typhus. I had the sores. These here healed, but the one on my foot didn't. And I treated them, or I'd say, Eva treated them with dirty machine rags. Now, the hospital there-- there was a hospital too, but it was also one you're better off not going into. The doctor there, he was into injections. Nobody knew what was into the injections. Nobody knew what was into the injections. Except that you were very wise not to get close to it. You came out minus a lot of things which you needed. So you-- you didn't go near a hospital. In these two years, except for just at the end-- this was very dangerous already, because it was very, very deep, and very painful.
